facts_disclosed: Kwabena back in school, scholarship 500 of 650 cedis, Kofi pays 150

turns.content: my friend. today I am a happy man. I had to tell someone
That's wonderful to hear! What happened?
my eldest son Kwabena. he had to leave school some time ago. it was a difficult thing. the fees were too much and the farm needed hands. but today he is going back
That must be such a relief for your whole family. Getting back into school after time away takes real determination. What made it possible for him to return?
the cooperative. we started a scholarship fund with the fair trade premium money. the members voted to set some aside for the children of farming families. Kwabena applied and they approved him
That's a fantastic use of the fair trade premium -- investing it directly back into the community's children. How much does the scholarship cover?
the term fees are 650 cedis. the scholarship covers 500. I pay the remaining 150 from my own pocket. 150 I can manage my friend. 650 was breaking me

That's a huge difference. 150 cedis is manageable where 650 was not -- and it means Kwabena gets his education back. The scholarship fund is doing exactly what it should.

Did you have to step aside from the decision since he's your son, being the chairman?

yes I did not vote on his application. the committee of 5 elders decided. I told them to judge him the same as any other child. if he does not deserve it then he does not get it. but they approved. his marks from before he left were good

That shows real integrity -- recusing yourself and letting the committee decide on merit. And the fact that Kwabena earned it on his own marks makes it even better. No one can say he got special treatment.

How is Kwabena feeling about going back? Sometimes it's hard for a young person to return after time away.

he is nervous. his classmates have moved ahead. but he is determined. he wakes at 5am now to study before farm work. I see the books open on the table every morning. 'the young tree that bends does not break my friend'

Waking at 5am to study on his own shows real maturity. The time on the farm probably gave him a deeper appreciation for what education means. Students who return after a break often end up more focused than they were before.

His classmates may be ahead in the syllabus, but he'll catch up if he keeps that discipline. Does the school have any support for students who've had a gap?
the headmaster said he can attend extra lessons on Saturday mornings. no charge. the headmaster is a good man. he knows our situation

That's a kind gesture from the headmaster -- Saturday extra lessons will help Kwabena close the gap quickly. Between the early morning studying and the extra sessions, he has a real plan for catching up.

How did Akosua take the news?

my friend. Akosua cried the day he put the uniform on again. she stood at the door watching him walk to school and the tears came. she did not even try to hide it. a mother's heart my friend. it knows things the mind cannot explain

That is a beautiful picture. Watching your child walk back to school in his uniform after you feared that chapter was closed -- I can understand why she couldn't hold it back. Those are the moments that make all the hard work and sacrifice worthwhile.

You should be proud of what you've built with the cooperative scholarship fund. It's not just helping Kwabena -- it will help other farming families' children too.

that is the purpose. 3 other children also received from the fund this term. the rain does not fall on one roof alone. this is what the cooperative is for. I will make sure the fund continues. Nyame has opened this door and we will walk through it

Four children back in school because the cooperative decided to invest in its future. That's a legacy that outlasts any single cocoa season. You've done something that will matter for years.

Give Kwabena encouragement from me. Tell him to keep those 5am study sessions going. Take care, and enjoy this good day.
I will tell him. medaase my friend. today the heart is light. good evening
Good evening. You've earned that lightness. Enjoy it with your family tonight.
